transcript.transcript: Combined, but the drivers all seemed to take it in their stride and took it as it did come. Then one day I was up on Copper Creek, On the other side of Copper Creek, where the logs went down, we went down the river, on the Coeur d'Alene River with them. That one year, they found a deer that had broken both of her legs, and they had to kill her, and she had two fawns. So the cook's wife loved baby bottles and they fed those two fawns with baby bottles and raised them all that summer. Those fawns was in the camp. They'd sleep under the benches and the man let all the men pet them And they had them there all that summer, and then that fall, when we closed up the camp, of course they just left the fawns out there for the winter. And when we went back that spring, one of them come back. The other one must have got killed or never showed up, but one of the fawns come back. Well, going back when we were up at Elk River, we had bears. We had five bears, one great big black one, and four brown bears. And all of us was by the cookhouse, because after the meals, They would take what garbage there was, which never there was. There never was too much garbage, because we had to cook the nut. The only thing you ever threw away was hotcakes, because you couldn't use them over. But oatmeal or anything that was left over, you'd put in the bread dough. Everything was used but the hotcakes. But the bones would come off the table and the garbage would come off the table. Of course, had to go up. They had made a big hole with a lid on it, and that's where they poured the garbage. And every night the bears would come on down there to eat on the garbage. And we would go up and watch the bears. They'd even come part way down. They would know at separate times when they heard the noise of the dishes. They'd start gathering up there and there's one or two of them, would come down the trail to meet that wheelbarrow as it would go on up. And then the bears would eat. But when that big black one come, the bears would stay on the side and let him eat first. Then when he was full, then they would take over. And they would get close enough to us that if we wanted to, we could have reached out and pet him. And no one can tell me that a bear can see. He'll stand up on his hind legs and smell. As long as we stood perfectly still, he'd smell all around us and then get down and go on to his business. And we never were afraid of any of them, but that big black one, We stayed a good distance from him. His fur was so thick that when the breeze went through it, it was just like wind in a field of wheat. It was just in ripples. He was a beautiful bear. Once in a while the cook would give us a watermelon and we'd set it out for the bears. And those bears, whichever one got that watermelon, he would take that watermelon and break it over a log or stump or whatever, which was closest. And then he'd take a chunk of that and sit and eat with his two paws out of that chunk of watermelon, just like we would eat a piece of watermelon. And did they ever love watermelons? And cantaloupes. We used to put cantaloupes out for them because we bought the stuff by the cases of the boxes. We thought he enjoyed the animals. It was always so nice to have the animals around. And then going back and, well, I want to go back where we had the horses. After we were up on Slaughterhouse, Mr. Johnson bought the teams, because in those days you didn't have no carts. You had to do everything with horses. And we had 46 head of horses, which would be 23 teams. And when the drivers and the fowl, we had no barns to keep the horses in and to keep them up here over the winter was kind of expensive because hay at them days was quite expensive. I think we paid all the way from $50 to $60 a ton for hay and I believe oats was $80 or $90 a ton. So the drivers would walk those horses from here clear down to the Palouse country to winnow them over in the fields down there. And then Johnson would bring the drivers home. And then in the spring he would take the drivers all down there. Each one would take his own team with a hodder and would walk those horses all the way back. They were great big draft horses, stood sixteen hands high. They looked a whole lot like those beer horses do now, and the drivers just, they were just too proud of backs. You didn't ride them, you walked them. And then anyway, the drivers wouldn't ride their horses. They thought a lot of their horses and they took care of them. And they never would overload their horses or would ever put anything on their backs excepting their harnesses. So they really took care of their horses. One time going back when they were up here on Drought City on the Coeur d'Alene River, Johnson, Herbert Heinemann was one of the drivers, which passed away this past winter, but he was one of our drivers, and Johnson was, they were taken back, the last team, they were taken off the river, And of course there was no, from Enidville up, there was no road them days. It was just a trail. And it was running right along the banks of the river. Well, maybe I don't know whether the trail that was going over part of the river or whether does, or whether the bank gave the team and Herbert Heinemann and the team went into the river. And it was deep enough that Herbert Heinemann had to stand in that river, arm-picked deep in that ice water. And he stood there for, I think it was between six and eight hours, holding those horses' heads up so they wouldn't drown, while Johnson went to camp to get help to come back that they could dig the bank out so they could get the horses out of the river. But that driver stayed with his team. He would not let them drown. So you see the Teamsters thought an awful lot of their horses. And then of course as the carts and all of that come on in. Well, we got rid of the horses and from then on everything went by machinery. And the latter years, even we got rid of the crosscut saws. The power saws come in and the first ones that come in were very big, heavy, clumsy saws but it was such an improvement over the other ones. But even with those saws, the sawyers still had to carry their bottle of kerosene to put on their blades when the pitch got, so the saw, if it cut the pitch so the saws could work in the logs. But now everything has changed so you never see Cox shoes anymore and each truck anymore seems to load his, has a loader on his own truck and loads them. But the latter years after Mr. Johnson got rid of all of his horses and most of his equipment, we bought the old armory building here in Coeur d'Alene where the armory used to be up on Best. And he went in to the trucking. We had our own trucks. They built, we sent back east for all the hardwood. They built all their bunks and everything right up here in that old armory. We built five jammers. They built them right from scratch and then put them on truck bodies and rebuilt those for the diamonds. And then Johnson took all the equipment up to the Diamond at Elk River. And from then on, it was all machinery. You done it with the trucks. And from that time, after that camp, he did run that camp. And then after that camp, Mr. Johnson went into just hauling. And at the time that Mr. Johnson quit, we were hauling up out at Clarkie for potlatch. The last ten years he had hauled for potlatch up at Clarkie. And when Mr. Johnson sold out, we sold 18 of those big 20-wheelers or whatever you call it, big trucks. And he sold them all when he quit, Mr. Johnson was in his 70s then when he quit logging. But he thoroughly enjoyed that life, and the family never wanted for anything. Every one or two years, the family had a brand new car, and for us it was a good life. And my children thoroughly enjoyed it. In the summer months we used to go to camp. When the children weren't in school, Mr. Johnson would take us up to camp and he always had a cabin that we could stay in. We eat our meals in the cookhouse, would ride around all over on the job for him. My son learned to drive to pick up in the woods for his dad. And then of course in the fall, we'd come home every two weeks to check on our home because we have our home here in town. And then in the fall, I'd have to come home to put the children in school. And I'm still in my home. I will have, I moved in this house in 43 and I'm still in my same home. And now I'm 83 years old. Elsie, that has been a most informative and interesting narrative that you've given us, and I have a couple of questions here I'd like to ask you before we finish this interview. What were some of your most memorable experiences during those years? Well, one year they took Mr. Johnson, the Luthos of Deer Park, got Mr. Johnson to go up at Nelson to be woods foreman up there. And I did enjoy that year up there because that country and the people are so much different from down here, But they are so far behind what we are. There's no refrigeration. They still had the wood stoves, no electric stoves, when we were on up there. But the people were, well, we were all like one big family. It was quite an experience to be up there. And I think I enjoyed the years when we had the camps, when we could go out. You knew all the men that worked for you. You knew them by their first names. They were all so friendly with my children and were also good to the children and I really enjoyed the summer months out in the woods. Well, Elsie, What do you expect to be the future of logging in North Idaho? We read so many controversial things about. Well, for the last twenty-five, thirty years, I've heard say the logs are all gone, there won't be much logging left. But they still seem to be logging. And of course in our day when we started out, white pine was all the meal box. But of course the latter use now, there's mixed timber And we even hauled trees down to four inches that they used for stilts that they sold to the mines. And then, of course, we sold an awful lot of cedar logs. Now, those would run all the way from 70 to 100 feet. And they would load them up to the top of the cab of the truck and then over the cab of the truck, the real long ones, went over the cab because coming down the road on those turns, they would scrape the turns as they went around. But we had many of those roads which they used for your telephone poles. Those were all cedar logs. But most of the cedar now, well, I guess they get way back in the woods, they can still find some cedars. But the real old virgin timber is mostly all gone. It's all the small stuff. And I suppose unless they preserve some of them, maybe the next 20 years there won't be no more timber. Well, Elsie, thank you for sharing these memorable, precious memories. Well, I really enjoyed being able. My memory isn't as good as it used to be, but I think I covered pretty much part of my life. You certainly have. You shared a lot of interesting things that many people have never heard about. Well thanks for asking me Sadie. I'm going to put the number here. And I'm going to put that in the class. And I can't get it. So what's the question? I see you're still doing it. You're just using it on your own. And you're all out of it. You're using it on your own. You're just using it on your own. You're just using it on your own. You're just using it on your own. I have one of these with a new logo. It's called, No Trouble. And this is the new logo. I have another one. It's called, No Invite Us. It's kind of neat. I have this one. It's called, No Problem. And I have this one. It's called, No Problem. It's got the same logo. I have one of this. It's called, No Offenders. I was going to say, it's good to see so many people. I'm sorry to be so distant, but thank you for not talking up. Thank you for your time. Thank you for your support. Thank you for your time. I'm glad people are here. I'm glad people are here. I'm glad that you're here. Thank you. I'm glad you're here. Thank you for your time. Thank you for having me. Thank you very much. I want people to know that I do this for all of the world, but this is for people that are going to be in the best world in the world. And I think that's why I do this very much. So I sit in the platform and I do the right thing. I don't do it for my money. I do it for God. God's the one that's going to go with time. And I'm going to go. God's the one that's going to help me. And the purpose is really to make you feel like you're up for the role of a city. And you feel like you need to be in it. And you feel like you need to be part of that little nightmare, and you need to go through that little nightmare. And you've got to give back. You've got to give in. And that's what you have to do. That's what you are in the book. And then you're going to do a round loop. So, you're going to do a double crochet. And then you're going to do a double crochet. Now, you're going to do a double crochet in the first stitch. And then we're going to go over. And we're going to make that a little bit stronger by doing this. And we're going to do it a little bit more. And we're going to start doing it I can't. No. I'm tired of this. I need to get this to some other person. You've got to be right. I'm afraid to listen to it. You seem like you're trying to help. I'm just trying to keep quiet, like you're telling me. I'm just like you. Like you're telling me. I'm tired of this. I'm tired. I'm tired of listening to it. I'm not going to listen. I am using the power of your spirit to reach out to the world within you. I am using the power of your spirit to make this world my own place. I am using the power of your spirit to make this world my own place.

lines.line: > This chapter recounts an experience on Copper Creek involving a deer with broken legs and the nurturing care of two fawns raised by the camp's cook and his wife. The fawns stayed at the camp for the summer and returned the next spring after being left to survive the winter on their own.
** Copper Creek, deer, fawns, nurturing, survival **
keywords: ** Copper Creek, deer, fawns, nurturing, survival **

metadata.title: On Copper Creek
metadata.synopsis: This chapter recounts an experience on Copper Creek involving a deer with broken legs and the nurturing care of two fawns raised by the camp's cook and his wife. The fawns stayed at the camp for the summer and returned the next spring after being left to survive the winter on their own.
metadata.keywords: Copper Creek, deer, fawns, nurturing, survival

lines.line: > The narrative describes interactions with bears near Elk River. Bears visited near the cookhouse to eat garbage. A notable hierarchy among the bears meant the big black bear would eat first. Human-animal interactions included watching the bears eat and occasionally giving them treats like watermelon.
** bears, Elk River, cookhouse, garbage, hierarchy **
keywords: ** bears, Elk River, cookhouse, garbage, hierarchy **

metadata.title: Bear Visits
metadata.synopsis: The narrative describes interactions with bears near Elk River. Bears visited near the cookhouse to eat garbage. A notable hierarchy among the bears meant the big black bear would eat first. Human-animal interactions included watching the bears eat and occasionally giving them treats like watermelon.
metadata.keywords: bears, Elk River, cookhouse, garbage, hierarchy

lines.line: > This section highlights the use of horses in logging operations, the care provided by Teamsters, and the logistical challenges of moving horses to and from the Palouse area. Additionally, it captures an incident involving horses slipping into the river and the subsequent rescue.
** horses, Teamsters, logistics, Coeur d'Alene River, rescue **
keywords: ** horses, Teamsters, logistics, Coeur d'Alene River, rescue **

metadata.title: Horse Logistics
metadata.synopsis: This section highlights the use of horses in logging operations, the care provided by Teamsters, and the logistical challenges of moving horses to and from the Palouse area. Additionally, it captures an incident involving horses slipping into the river and the subsequent rescue.

lines.line: > This chapter covers the transition from horse-driven to machine-driven logging operations. It details the arrival of heavy power saws and the subsequent phasing out of horses and crosscut saws. Mr. Johnson's transition into the trucking industry and the sale of logging equipment are also discussed.
** machinery transition, power saws, trucking, logging equipment, Mr. Johnson **
keywords: ** machinery transition, power saws, trucking, logging equipment, Mr. Johnson **

metadata.title: Transition to Machinery
metadata.synopsis: This chapter covers the transition from horse-driven to machine-driven logging operations. It details the arrival of heavy power saws and the subsequent phasing out of horses and crosscut saws. Mr. Johnson's transition into the trucking industry and the sale of logging equipment are also discussed.

lines.line: > A personal account of family life during summers spent in logging camps. It highlights the blending of work and family, with children learning and participating in camp activities, and the significance of maintaining connections with the community workers.
** logging camps, family life, community, summer, Mr. Johnson **
keywords: ** logging camps, family life, community, summer, Mr. Johnson **

lines.line: # Memorable Experiences and Future of Logging

> The interview ends with a discussion on memorable experiences in logging, emphasizing the community and camaraderie within the camps. It also touches on the predicted decline of logging due to diminishing timber resources versus ongoing operations.
** memorable experiences, future of logging, community, timber resources, decline **
